Who is the author of 'The Last Lesson'?
What is the name of the main character (student) in 'The Last Lesson'?
Which country occupies Alsace during the events of 'The Last Lesson'?
Who is M. Hamel in 'The Last Lesson'?
Why does Franz arrive late to school on that particular day?
What new regulation has been issued in Alsace?
options: Only German language to be taught in schools

questions.question: What is the last lesson to be taught in French by M. Hamel?
How does Franz feel at the beginning of the story?
Who attends M. Hamel's last lesson besides the students?
What does Franz notice about M. Hamel's appearance in the lesson?
options: He is wearing his special coat and embroidered collar
He is dressed as a German officer

questions.question: What realization dawns on Franz during the lesson?
options: The importance of learning one's own language
That he should have studied harder
That he will become a teacher

questions.question: What does M. Hamel reveal about his feelings at the end?
options: He is heartbroken at having to stop teaching French
He is excited about the change

questions.question: What is the theme of 'The Last Lesson'?
options: The value of education and national identity

questions.question: What does Franz regret during the lesson?
options: Not having studied French seriously before

questions.question: How is the story a critique of colonial occupation?
options: It shows how language and culture are suppressed by occupying forces
It shows the cruelty of German soldiers

questions.question: What literary device is used when Franz's indifference to learning contrasts with his later realization?
What does the notice on the schoolhouse door announce?
options: That only German language will be taught

questions.question: Which of the following best describes Franz's emotional journey in the story?
options: From indifference to awakening and regret

questions.question: What does M. Hamel's emotional breakdown at the end symbolize?
options: The loss of cultural identity under occupation
His personal failure as a teacher
The victory of the occupying force

questions.question: How does Daudet use the classroom setting to reflect the broader political situation?
options: The classroom mirrors the nation losing its language and identity
The classroom shows the success of the new regime
The classroom demonstrates advanced German education
The classroom is unaffected by politics

questions.question: What is the significance of the church bells in the story?
options: They mark the end of German occupation
They announce the last French lesson

questions.question: Why does old Hauser attend the last lesson?
options: He wants to learn French one more time before it's forbidden

questions.question: What is the central conflict in 'The Last Lesson'?
options: External: occupation forces vs. internal: personal awakening about the value of native language

questions.question: What does Franz's character development suggest about human nature?
options: We often fail to appreciate what we have until we stand to lose it
Education is a waste of time

questions.question: Who is the author of 'Lost Spring'?
What is the primary setting of 'Lost Spring'?
options: Firozabad, a glass-making city in India

questions.question: What is the main occupation of people in Firozabad?
Who are the main child characters introduced in the essay?
What is Mukesh's dream?
options: To work in the glass factory

questions.question: What does Mukesh's father do for a living?
Why is Mukesh's dream of becoming a mechanic significant?
options: It represents his desire to break free from the vicious cycle of poverty and family occupation
It shows he wants to earn more money
It indicates he doesn't respect his family
It means he will leave Firozabad

questions.question: Who is Saheb in the essay?
options: A street child who makes his living by rag-picking

questions.question: What is the significance of Saheb's name?
options: It means 'master' or 'sir' - ironic for someone in such poverty
It is just a common name
It is a term of disrespect

questions.question: Where does Saheb originally come from?
options: He is a refugee from Bangladesh
He is from a neighboring village

questions.question: What does Saheb find along the railway track?
options: Discarded items, rags, and useful materials to sell

questions.question: What is the irony in Saheb's situation?
options: He is free from the shackles of childhood labor in factories but enslaved to the streets
He is rich but pretends to be poor
He is educated but works as a rag-picker
He is from a wealthy family

questions.question: What does Anees Jung criticize in the essay?
options: The system that perpetuates child labor and poverty

questions.question: What is the 'lost spring' in the title referring to?
options: The lost childhood of children trapped in poverty and labor
A lost opportunity for the factory

questions.question: How does Jung use contrast in the essay?
options: Contrasting the dreams and hopes of children with the grim reality of their circumstances

questions.question: What is the generational cycle that Jung depicts?
options: Parents trapped in glass-making pass the curse to their children despite dreams of escape

questions.question: How does Mukesh's family view his ambition to be a mechanic?
options: They dismiss it as unrealistic given their financial situation
They help him find a job

questions.question: What social issue does 'Lost Spring' highlight?
options: Child labor, poverty, and the lack of social mobility in India

questions.question: Why does Saheb stop smiling after getting a job at a tea stall?
options: He realizes he has traded one form of slavery for another with fixed wages and rules
He is unhappy with his employer
He wants to return to rag-picking

questions.question: What is Jung's perspective on the children's situation?
options: Sympathetic and critical of the systems that trap them

questions.question: What literary device does Jung use to highlight the absurdity of child labor?
options: Irony - contrast between innocent dreams and harsh reality

questions.question: What does the glass-making industry symbolize in the essay?
options: The vicious cycle of poverty that traps families across generations

questions.question: How does Anees Jung make her essay a call for social change?
options: By revealing the human cost of poverty and child labor through individual stories

questions.question: What is the significance of focusing on Mukesh specifically?
options: His determination despite obstacles represents hope, yet his constraints show structural injustice

questions.question: What is Jung's overall message through 'Lost Spring'?
options: Children deserve childhood, education, and freedom from labor regardless of economic circumstances
Children should work to support families

questions.question: Who is the author of 'Deep Water'?
What is the main subject of 'Deep Water'?
options: The author's personal struggle with fear of water and his journey to overcome it

questions.question: At what age did Douglas develop a fear of water?
options: Around age six, after a traumatic incident in the Yakima River
He was born with the fear

questions.question: What incident caused Douglas's initial fear of water?
options: He was knocked down by a wave and nearly drowned in the Yakima River
He was pushed into water as a prank

questions.question: How did the fear affect Douglas's life?
options: It dominated his thoughts and prevented him from enjoying water-related activities
It only affected him at night

questions.question: What does Douglas eventually do to overcome his fear?
options: He systematically learns to swim with a coach named Sidley

questions.question: Who is Sidley in the essay?
options: Douglas's swimming coach who helps him conquer his fear

questions.question: What technique does Sidley use to teach Douglas?
options: Gradual exposure and confidence-building exercises in shallow water
Throwing him in the deep end
Forcing him to swim long distances

questions.question: What psychological insight does Douglas offer about fear?
options: Fear is a phantom that loses its power when confronted directly and systematically

questions.question: How long does it take Douglas to overcome his fear?
options: Several years of consistent effort and practice

questions.question: What does Douglas say about the nature of his fear?
options: It was largely psychological and self-imposed
It was based on real danger

questions.question: What is the turning point in Douglas's journey?
options: When he realizes he can touch the bottom of the pool and begins to feel in control
When he wins a swimming race
When he moves to a different location

questions.question: What is the broader theme of 'Deep Water'?
options: Personal courage involves confronting one's deepest fears through determination and effort

questions.question: How does Douglas use his personal narrative as a lesson?
options: To show that anyone can overcome psychological barriers with proper help and persistence

questions.question: What does Douglas discover about confidence?
options: Confidence grows gradually through small successes and repeated exposure

questions.question: What role does determination play in Douglas's success?
options: It is essential; without persistence, he would have given up on learning to swim
Determination is for young people only

questions.question: How does Douglas describe his mental state while learning to swim?
options: He experiences internal conflict between his fear and his determination to succeed

questions.question: What does the title 'Deep Water' symbolize?
options: A metaphor for facing deep-seated fears and psychological challenges

questions.question: What does Douglas say about the relationship between fear and danger?
options: Fear often magnifies perceived danger beyond actual risk
Fear is always proportional to danger
There is no relationship between them
Danger causes fear only in weak people

questions.question: How does Douglas's essay relate to human resilience?
options: It demonstrates that humans have the capacity to overcome obstacles through effort and support
Only exceptional people can be resilient

questions.question: What is significant about Douglas's willingness to be vulnerable in his narrative?
options: It makes his story more relatable and powerful; admitting fear shows strength, not weakness
It is irrelevant to the story

questions.question: What does Douglas ultimately learn about himself through this experience?
options: That he is capable of far more than his fears suggested, and that limitations are often self-imposed

questions.question: How does the essay serve as an inspiration to readers?
options: By showing that persistent effort can overcome even deep-rooted psychological obstacles
By proving that fear is good

questions.question: What is the connection between Douglas's fear and his eventual mastery?
options: The same intensity that drove his fear motivated him to conquer it thoroughly

questions.question: What message does Douglas convey about seeking help?
options: Asking for proper guidance and coaching is crucial to overcoming significant challenges
Help is a sign of weakness

questions.question: Who is the author of 'The Rattrap'?
What is the profession of the main character at the beginning of the story?
options: A vagabond and peddler of rattraps

questions.question: What is the name of the main character?
options: Nils Kajsa (not explicitly given, referred to as the peddler)

questions.question: What is the peddler's philosophy about the world?
options: The world is a rattrap that traps people through their desires and weaknesses
The world is a fair place

questions.question: Why does the peddler compare the world to a rattrap?
options: Because he believes people are lured by temptations and baited into traps of their own making
Because the world is literally dangerous
Because he is angry at society

questions.question: What does the peddler steal from the iron works?
options: Money/thirty kronor that he finds lying around

questions.question: Why is it significant that the peddler steals money from the iron works?
options: He falls into his own rattrap by yielding to temptation, proving his philosophy about the world
He is trying to become rich
He wants to test his beliefs

questions.question: Where does the peddler go after stealing the money?
options: He runs away and hides in the forest

questions.question: Who is Edla Willmansson?
options: The compassionate daughter of the ironmaster who takes pity on the peddler
A servant at the iron works

questions.question: Why does Edla invite the peddler into her home?
options: Out of compassion and Christian charity, seeing the good in him despite his destitute appearance
She wants to turn him in to the police

questions.question: What is the peddler's initial reaction to Edla's kindness?
options: He is suspicious and cannot believe someone would be kind to him without ulterior motive

questions.question: How does Edla convince the peddler to stay with them for Christmas?
options: She treats him with genuine warmth and respect, calling him Captain, which restores his dignity

questions.question: What transformation occurs in the peddler during his stay with the Willmansson family?
options: He begins to believe in human goodness and experiences redemption and change of heart
He decides to steal from them

questions.question: What does the peddler decide to do with the stolen money?
options: He returns it to the iron works, showing his genuine change of character

questions.question: What is the significance of the peddler returning the money?
options: It demonstrates that human kindness and compassion can redeem even the most hardened cynics
It is a test of Edla

questions.question: How does the story challenge the peddler's rattrap philosophy?
options: Edla's kindness proves that the world is not entirely a rattrap; goodness and redemption are possible
The story supports the rattrap idea

questions.question: What role does appearance play in the story?
options: The peddler's shabby appearance hides his humanity; Edla sees past it and treats him with respect

questions.question: What does Lagerlöf suggest about human nature through this story?
options: People are capable of both cynicism and redemption; compassion has transformative power

questions.question: How does Christmas symbolism function in the story?
options: Christmas represents hope, redemption, and the power of goodness, contrasting with the peddler's cynicism
Christmas is irrelevant to the plot

questions.question: What is the central conflict in 'The Rattrap'?
options: Internal: the peddler's cynicism vs. external evidence of human goodness that challenges his worldview

questions.question: What does the title 'The Rattrap' ultimately represent?
options: Both a literal trap for rats and a metaphor for how people entrap themselves through cynicism and despair
Just a device for catching rats

questions.question: How does the peddler's character arc demonstrate growth?
options: From a cynical, self-centered man to someone capable of trust, gratitude, and moral choice

questions.question: What is Lagerlöf's critique through this story?
options: Society judges people by appearance and status rather than character, but individual kindness can overcome this

questions.question: Why is Edla's role crucial to the story's message?
options: She embodies the transformative power of unconditional kindness and faith in human goodness
She is just a minor character

questions.question: What does the story ultimately suggest about redemption?
options: Redemption is possible for anyone when met with genuine human kindness and compassion
Only the wealthy can be redeemed

questions.question: What is the main subject of 'Indigo'?
options: Gandhi's campaign against indigo plantations and the indigo farmers' struggle in Champaran

questions.question: Where does the indigo plantation struggle take place?
Why were indigo farmers important to the British colonial economy?
options: They were forced to grow indigo, which was valuable for dye exports

questions.question: What system was used by the British plantation owners to exploit the indigo farmers?
options: The tinkathia system: a five-year contract forcing farmers to grow indigo on a portion of their land

questions.question: What was Gandhi's role in the Champaran indigo issue?
options: He led a campaign to expose and abolish the exploitative tinkathia system

questions.question: When did Gandhi arrive in Champaran?

12.topic: On the Face of It (Susan Hill) - Vistas

questions.question: Who are the two main characters in 'On the Face of It'?
What is Derry's defining characteristic?
options: He has a burned face, causing him social rejection

options: He has lost his sight and uses a white stick

questions.question: Where does the story take place?
options: In Mr. Lamb's garden and orchard

options: He is accepting and has found ways to live a full life

questions.question: What is Derry's initial expectation about his scars?
options: They define him as monstrous and unacceptable to society

options: Acceptance and the possibility of overcoming physical disability and social stigma

questions.question: How does Mr. Lamb's garden function in the story?
options: It is a symbol of possibility, growth, and acceptance despite limitations

questions.question: What does Mr. Lamb teach Derry through his life example?
options: Disability does not define one's ability to live fully and help others

questions.question: What is Derry's reaction to Mr. Lamb initially?
options: He is cautious but gradually opens up

options: Based on surface appearance only, but deeper truths lie beneath

options: Wisdom and acceptance come with age, helping him navigate his condition

options: The garden setting symbolizes growth, possibility, and a safe space for acceptance

options: It shows disabled individuals as full, capable, and living meaningful lives

options: Mentoring and companionable, with mutual benefit

questions.question: What does Mr. Lamb's conversation reveal about his character?
options: He is philosophically balanced, accepting of life, and genuinely interested in others

questions.question: What is the significance of Derry's gradual openness?

questions.question: What does the play suggest about the nature of disability?
options: It is a difference that can be accommodated through acceptance and adaptation

questions.question: What is Mr. Lamb's philosophy on life?
options: Life is valuable despite challenges, and can be lived fully with a positive attitude

questions.question: What is the ultimate impact of Mr. Lamb on Derry?
options: He offers a model of resilience and acceptance that may help Derry envision a fuller life

questions.question: What is the relevance of 'On the Face of It' to contemporary society?
options: It addresses ongoing issues of acceptance, disability discrimination, and the human capacity to overcome prejudice

12.topic: Evans Tries an O-Level (Colin Dexter) - Vistas

Childhood is irrelevant to adult life
Childhood experiences are all the same

questions.question: What is the ultimate message of 'Memories of Childhood'?
